# [Scientific Name] — [Common Name / Abbreviation]

> **Accepted Latin name:** [Genus species Author]
> **Common names:** [Name 1], [Name 2], [Name 3]
> **Botanical family:** [Family name]
> **Type:** Fungi / Plant
> **Used part:** [root / fruiting body / leaf / seed / bark / etc.]
> **Preparation/extract type:** [aqueous extract / ethanolic extract / standardized extract / etc.]
> **Taxonomy status:** Accepted per POWO / Index Fungorum [date checked]
> **Last reviewed:** [YYYY-MM-DD]

**Research categories:**
- [Category 1 — e.g. Immunomodulation]
- [Category 2 — e.g. Adaptogen]
- [Category 3 — e.g. Hepatoprotection]

**Evidence level (highest available):**
- [ ] A — Systematic review / meta-analysis of human RCTs
- [ ] B — Human RCT(s)
- [ ] C — Controlled or observational human studies
- [ ] D — Preclinical (animal) evidence only
- [ ] E — In vitro / mechanistic only
- [ ] T — Traditional use only
- [ ] U — Insufficient / unknown

> **Medical disclaimer:** This profile is for informational and educational research purposes only. It does not constitute medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment. All pharmacological claims should be independently verified by consulting the primary cited literature. No compound listed is approved to prevent, treat, diagnose, or cure any disease.

---

## NARRATIVE SUMMARY

### HISTORICAL USE

[Describe documented traditional and historical uses of this plant or fungus. Include the cultural or geographical origin of use, traditional practitioners involved, traditional preparations, and any historical records. Cite ethnobotanical or historical sources where available. Label as traditional use — not clinical evidence.]

**Evidence type:** Traditional / ethnobotanical — not clinical evidence

### MODERN RESEARCH CONTEXT

[Summarize the modern scientific research on this species. Describe the primary areas of investigation, the quality and volume of evidence, and what the strongest current evidence supports. Distinguish clearly between human clinical findings, animal study findings, and in vitro findings. Do not overstate what the evidence shows.]

**Highest evidence level:** [SR-MA / RCT / CCT / OBS / ANIMAL / IN-VITRO / TRAD — for each studied indication]

### ADVERSE EFFECTS

[List documented adverse effects from human studies, case reports, and post-marketing surveillance. Include: frequency if known, severity, reversibility, and the evidence source for each effect. Do not write "no adverse effects" if data is simply missing — write "Insufficient clinical data on adverse effects".]

Common (≥10% incidence): [or "Not established"]
Uncommon (1–10%): [or "Not established"]
Rare (<1%) / Case reports: [list with source type]
Unknown incidence: [list]

### CONTRAINDICATIONS

[List all documented and precautionary contraindications. Include: specific medical conditions, concurrent medications, known hypersensitivity, and populations where use should be avoided. If contraindications are precautionary rather than evidence-based, label them as such.]

- [Contraindication 1] — [Evidence basis: case report / precautionary / clinical trial]
- [Contraindication 2]
- Autoimmune conditions: [if applicable]
- Pre-surgery: [if applicable, specify washout period if known]

---

## PHYTOCHEMICAL DATA

### USED PART

[Specify exactly which part of the plant or fungus is used in the clinical evidence. Distinguish clearly if different parts have different evidence profiles. For fungi: distinguish fruiting body from mycelium — these have different chemical profiles and the distinction matters clinically.]

**Primary part used in studies:** [e.g. Fruiting body / Root / Leaf]
**Other parts studied:** [if applicable]
**Note on preparation:** [e.g. Hot water extraction / ethanolic extraction — relevant to bioavailability of specific compounds]

### PRIMARY ACTIVE COMPOUNDS

[List the primary bioactive compounds with their compound classes. Include concentration ranges from standardized extracts where available. For each compound, note the type: polysaccharide, terpenoid, alkaloid, flavonoid, saponin, etc.]

- **[Compound 1]** — [Class] — [Role / mechanism note if established]
- **[Compound 2]** — [Class]
- **[Compound 3]** — [Class]

---

## MECHANISM OF ACTION

[Describe the pharmacological mechanisms at the molecular and cellular level. Highlight key receptor targets, enzyme systems, signaling pathways, or immune system interactions. Use `**bold**` for key pharmacological targets (e.g., **NF-κB**, **TLR-4**, **COX-2**). Distinguish between mechanisms established in human tissue/clinical studies vs. those established only in animal or cell models. Label evidence source for each mechanism claim.]

Key targets and pathways:
- **[TARGET 1]** — [mechanism] — [Evidence: human / animal / in vitro]
- **[TARGET 2]** — [mechanism] — [Evidence: human / animal / in vitro]

---

## PHARMACOKINETICS

> **Note:** Unless stated otherwise, pharmacokinetic data below refers to [human / animal / in vitro] studies. Extrapolation from animal or in vitro data to human pharmacokinetics is not established without human PK studies.

### ABSORPTION

[Describe absorption characteristics: oral bioavailability, rate of absorption, food effects, factors affecting absorption. Specify the compound(s) the data applies to, extract type used in the study, and whether data is from human or preclinical studies. If no human PK data exists, state this explicitly.]

### DISTRIBUTION

[Describe tissue distribution, protein binding, volume of distribution. Specify data source. Note if distribution data is only available from animal studies or in vitro models.]

### METABOLISM

[Describe metabolic pathways: which CYP450 enzymes are involved (if known), primary metabolites, enterohepatic circulation. Note if this creates herb–drug interaction risks via CYP450 modulation.]

### EXCRETION

[Describe elimination route (renal, biliary, fecal), half-life of active compounds, and any dose accumulation considerations. Note data source.]

---

## DRUG INTERACTIONS

[Document all known or suspected herb–drug interactions. For each interaction, specify: mechanism (pharmacokinetic or pharmacodynamic), severity (contraindicated / major / moderate / minor), evidence level, and recommended action.]

> Even limited evidence of interaction should be flagged. Lack of data does not mean no interaction exists.

**Pharmacokinetic interactions (CYP450 / P-glycoprotein):**
- [Drug class or specific drug] — [Mechanism: CYP3A4 inhibition / induction / etc.] — [Severity] — [Evidence: clinical / case report / in vitro]

**Pharmacodynamic interactions:**
- [Drug class] — [Mechanism: additive / synergistic / antagonistic effect on what system] — [Severity] — [Evidence]

**Anticoagulant / antiplatelet interactions:**
- [Specific drugs: warfarin, aspirin, clopidogrel, etc. if relevant] — [Evidence]

If no interactions are documented: "No clinically significant herb–drug interactions have been formally established. Precautionary interactions based on pharmacology include: [list]. Monitor closely if used concurrently with [drug classes]."

---

## TOXICITY

[Summarize acute and chronic toxicity data. Include LD50 values from animal studies if available (label as animal data). Describe any organ-specific toxicity findings. Note any reports of hepatotoxicity, nephrotoxicity, or carcinogenicity. Include maximum studied human doses where available.]

**LD50 (animal data):** [value + species + route or "Not established"]
**Chronic toxicity:** [findings or "No long-term human safety data available"]
**Hepatotoxicity risk:** [evidence or "Case reports of hepatotoxicity exist / None reported / Unknown"]
**Maximum dose studied in humans:** [or "Not established"]

---

## SPECIAL PRECAUTIONS

### PREGNANCY

[State clearly whether use is safe, contraindicated, or simply unstudied during pregnancy. Do not write "probably safe" without human evidence. If insufficient data exists, write: "Insufficient safety data during pregnancy. Use is not recommended due to lack of established safety."]

Evidence basis: [Human clinical data / Animal data only / No data — traditional avoidance]

### LACTATION

[Same approach as pregnancy. Specify whether the compound or its metabolites are excreted in breast milk if known.]

Evidence basis: [specify]

### HEPATIC IMPAIRMENT

[Address use in patients with liver disease. Note if hepatic metabolism of active compounds is relevant. Flag any hepatotoxic potential.]

### RENAL IMPAIRMENT

[Address use in patients with kidney disease. Note if renally excreted compounds may accumulate. Specify whether dose adjustment is recommended.]

---

## EVIDENCE ASSESSMENT

### Human Evidence

[Summarize the strongest human clinical evidence. For each studied indication, provide: study type, sample size (approximate), duration, dose used, extract type, and main outcome. Note consistency between studies.]

| Indication | Study type | n | Duration | Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [Indication 1] | [RCT/CCT/OBS] | [n=] | [weeks] | [finding] |
| [Indication 2] | | | | |

### Preclinical Evidence

[Summarize relevant animal and in vitro findings. Label clearly as preclinical. Note which findings may translate to humans and which remain unconfirmed.]

### Traditional Use

[Describe traditional use without implying it equals clinical efficacy. Label as historical/ethnobotanical evidence.]

### Evidence Limitations

[List the main limitations of the evidence base: small sample sizes, lack of standardization, heterogeneous preparations, short duration, industry funding, etc.]

- [Limitation 1]
- [Limitation 2]
- [Limitation 3]

## KEY SOURCES

[List all cited references in numbered format. Every claim in this profile must be traceable to one of these sources. Include PMID or DOI for all literature references.]

[1] — [Author(s). Title. Journal. Year;Volume(Issue):Pages. PMID: XXXXXXXX]
[2] — [Author(s). Title. Journal. Year. PMID: XXXXXXXX]
[3] — [Official monograph source. Publisher. Year. URL if available.]

**Preferred source types (in order of priority):**
1. Systematic reviews and meta-analyses (PubMed)
2. Randomized controlled trials (PubMed)
3. EMA/HMPC monographs
4. ESCOP monographs
5. WHO monographs on selected medicinal plants
6. NIH/NCCIH fact sheets
7. Cochrane reviews
8. Animal studies (labeled as preclinical)
9. In vitro studies (labeled as mechanistic)

## DISCLAIMER

All profiles published in BMS Archive are based on peer-reviewed literature or credible scientific and regulatory sources. This database is for informational and research purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. The information provided does not replace professional medical consultation. No compound listed has been approved by any regulatory authority to prevent, treat, diagnose, or cure any disease.

Users must independently verify all pharmacological claims by consulting the primary cited literature before making any health decisions. BMS Archive holds no liability for any clinical decisions made on the basis of this information.
